title: Saturday at the Market

story.location: A WhatsApp conversation the morning after the family dinner
story.scene: Your phone buzzes. Carlos is checking in after dinner and wants to take you to his favorite market on Saturday.
story.mission: Today you’ll arrange a Saturday morning trip to the market with Carlos.
story.continuity: Carlos asks how your family is before inviting you out, naturally reusing greetings, family language, and the conversation from dinner.

emotionalArc.startsWith: The learner sees an unexpected message from Carlos after the family dinner.
emotionalArc.endsWith: The learner is looking forward to a real Saturday plan in Madrid.
emotionalArc.earnedBy: Understanding the message and arranging the market visit in Spanish.

microCliffhanger.speaker: Carlos
microCliffhanger.spanish: Antes de vernos, necesito comprar algo especial en el mercado... Mañana te cuento.
microCliffhanger.english: Before we meet, I need to buy something special at the market... I’ll tell you tomorrow.

carlosIntroduction.title: Make a plan with me
carlosIntroduction.message: ¡Buenos días! Yesterday you met my family, and they really enjoyed meeting you. This Saturday there’s a wonderful market in Madrid. Let’s make some plans together. By the end, you’ll be able to ask when someone is free, choose a day and time, and agree on a plan.
carlosIntroduction.prompt: Open my message and answer: Sí, estoy libre.

canDo: Say what day works for me.
Accept a plan or suggest another time.

recycledLanguage.use: Check in after the family dinner before making plans.

objectives: Understand a short message about weekend plans.
Use days and parts of the day to arrange a meeting.
Agree on Saturday at ten as one complete, useful plan.

essentialPhrases.usage: Accept by saying you are available.
Use ocupado if you identify as masculine and ocupada if you identify as feminine.
Ask for the day or time of a plan.
essentialPhrases.exampleEnglish: When are we going to the market?

essentialPhrases.usage: Use el before a day when saying when something happens.
Learn this as one complete meeting-time phrase for now.
A natural way to accept a suggestion.
essentialPhrases.exampleSpanish: El sábado por la mañana. Perfecto.

essentialPhrases.usage: Close the conversation after the plan is settled.

vocabulary.tip: Spanish days are written with lowercase letters.
Use el martes for on Tuesday.
Keep the written accent over é.
The j has a strong breathy Spanish sound.
Viernes comes before sábado.
The written accent marks the first syllable: SÁ-ba-do.
Use el domingo for on Sunday.
Use hoy for the current day.
Context distinguishes mañana, tomorrow, from la mañana, the morning.
Esta agrees with the feminine word semana.
Treat fin de semana as one useful chunk.
Use ahora for right now.
Después can suggest doing something later.
Use antes to suggest an earlier time.
Por la mañana means in the morning.
Spanish la tarde covers the afternoon and part of the evening.
Por la noche means at night.
Libre does not change for masculine or feminine here.
Choose ocupado or ocupada to match the speaker.
In this lesson, use it to talk about a meeting time.
This is where you and Carlos will go in Lesson 5.
You will hear it in ¿Quieres ir...?, a useful invitation chunk.
vocabulary.exampleEnglish: Do you want to go to the market?

vocabulary.tip: Use venir when someone comes with you or to you.
In Spain, quedar is common for arranging to meet.
Learn podemos as a useful chunk meaning we can.
The written accent distinguishes yes from si, if.
A warm, natural way to agree.
Use it when a suggested plan works for you.

grammar.meaning: Say when a plan happens without building a full clock-time system.
grammar.explanation: Use el + day for a plan: el sábado. Use por la mañana, por la tarde, or por la noche for a broad part of the day. Ask ¿Cuándo? when you need to know when. For this mission, learn a las diez as one complete phrase meaning at ten.

notes.title: Use el with a day
notes.text: Say el sábado for on Saturday. Days stay lowercase in Spanish.

notes.title: Use por for a part of the day
notes.text: Say por la mañana, por la tarde, or por la noche when an exact hour is not important.

notes.title: Ask one powerful question
notes.text: ¿Cuándo? can ask for either a day or a time: When?

notes.title: Keep the meeting time together
notes.text: Treat a las diez as one useful chunk today. A later lesson can explain the full clock system.

grammar.practicePrompt: Ask Carlos when, then choose a day and a part of the day.

dialogue.title: A message from Carlos
dialogue.setting: The morning after the family dinner, Carlos messages you to arrange Saturday’s market visit.

lines.speaker: Carlos
lines.english: Hi, Tom! How is your family?

lines.speaker: Learner
lines.spanish: ¡Hola, Carlos! Mi familia está muy bien.
lines.english: Hi, Carlos! My family is very well.

lines.speaker: Carlos
lines.english: That’s great! Are you free this Saturday?

lines.speaker: Carlos
lines.english: Perfect! Do you want to go to the market?

comprehension.prompt: Where does Carlos want to go?

pronunciation.focus: Mission phrases, clear rhythm, and Spanish question melody
tips: Keep ¿Estás libre? as one smooth thought group.
Stress the first syllable in sábado: SÁ-ba-do.
In las diez, connect the final s lightly into diez.
Keep every vowel short and clear in perfecto.

items.note: Let your voice rise gently for the yes-or-no question.
Say the three words as one time chunk.
Keep both words even and relaxed.
Keep the ñ sound in mañana clear.

listening.title: Carlos’s WhatsApp message
listening.instructions: Listen once at natural speed. Replay slowly if needed, then answer without translating every word.
listening.naturalScript: Hola, Tom. ¡Qué bien lo pasamos ayer! ¿Cómo está tu familia? ¿Estás libre este sábado? Podemos ir al mercado juntos. ¿A las diez de la mañana?
listening.slowScript: Hola, Tom. / ¡Qué bien lo pasamos ayer! / ¿Cómo está tu familia? / ¿Estás libre / este sábado? / Podemos ir / al mercado / juntos. / ¿A las diez / de la mañana?

options: Are you at the market today?
We can go to the market together.
items.answer: We can go to the market together.

listeningPhrases.english: We can go to the market together.
listeningPhrases.note: Podemos proposes something you can do together.
Hear a las diez as one meeting-time chunk.

speaking.instructions: Move from copying Carlos to arranging a plan that fits your own life.

items.focus: Keep the question smooth and connected.
Say a las diez as one chunk.

items.prompt: Carlos asks: ¿Estás libre este sábado? Accept the invitation.
items.focus: Answer without reading if you can.

items.prompt: Ask Carlos when the plan is.
Carlos asks: ¿Cuándo estás libre normalmente? Give a true day and part of the day.
items.text: Estoy libre el sábado por la mañana.
items.focus: Change sábado and mañana to fit your life.

miniConversation.setting: Carlos is choosing the best time for your market visit.
miniConversation.goal: Choose a day and part of the day, respond to Carlos’s follow-up, and confirm the plan.

turns.speaker: Carlos
turns.english: Hi! Which day do you prefer?

turns.speaker: Carlos
turns.spanish: ¿Por la mañana o por la tarde?
turns.english: In the morning or in the afternoon?

turns.speaker: Learner
turns.intent: Accept or say you are busy and suggest another part of the day.

culture.title: Making plans in Spain
culture.text: Social plans in Spain may begin later than learners expect, especially in the evening. Friends commonly confirm by message the same day or the night before. Once a plan is set, nos vemos is a natural everyday way to say see you. Times vary by person and region, so follow the specific time you agree on rather than assuming.

nativeSpeech.note: Useful to recognize; ¿Estás libre el sábado? is the simpler phrase to produce now.
Vale is especially common in Spain. Perfecto is neutral and works widely.

commonMistakes.why: Use el before a day when saying when a plan happens.
Por la mañana is the core phrase taught here for in the morning.
Use ¿Cuándo? to ask when a plan happens.
Spanish uses estar with libre and ocupado in this situation.

canDo: I can understand a short message proposing a plan.
I can ask and answer whether someone is free.
I can choose a day and part of the day.
I can confirm a simple meeting time and close with nos vemos.
review.conversationReturn: When Carlos asks about your availability later, answer with a real day and part of the day.

quiz.explanation: Libre means free or available in this planning question.
The sequence is jueves, viernes, sábado, domingo.
Estoy ocupado or ocupada tells someone you are busy.
Mañana means tomorrow; la mañana means the morning.

quiz.prompt: Which phrase means in the morning?
quiz.explanation: Por la mañana is the core phrase for in the morning.
¿Estás libre? asks if someone is available.
Use el before a day when saying when something happens.

quiz.prompt: Carlos asks ¿Cuándo? Choose a complete useful answer.
quiz.explanation: ¿Cuándo? asks when, so answer with a day or time expression.

quiz.prompt: Carlos suggests a time that works for you. What is the most natural response?
quiz.explanation: Sí, perfecto naturally accepts a proposed plan.
Nos vemos is see you; entonces means then in this context.

quiz.prompt: You are busy in the morning but free later. What can you say?
options: Estoy ocupado por la mañana. Podemos ir después.
Estoy libre por la mañana. Nos vemos antes.
Soy de Madrid. Vivo en Granada.
quiz.answer: Estoy ocupado por la mañana. Podemos ir después.
quiz.explanation: The first sentence declines the morning; the second suggests later.

quiz.prompt: Which option is a part of the day?
quiz.explanation: La tarde means the afternoon or early evening.

quiz.prompt: Carlos writes: ¿Estás libre este sábado? Podemos ir al mercado juntos. What is he doing?
options: Inviting you to make a market plan
quiz.answer: Inviting you to make a market plan
quiz.explanation: He asks whether you are free and proposes going to the market together.

quiz.prompt: Put this exchange in a natural order. 1 Sí, perfecto. 2 ¿A las diez? 3 ¿Estás libre el sábado? 4 Sí, estoy libre.
quiz.explanation: First ask about availability, accept, suggest a time, then confirm it.

quiz.prompt: Translate: Estoy libre el domingo por la tarde.
options: I’m at the market Sunday night.
quiz.explanation: Libre means free, el domingo is Sunday, and por la tarde is in the afternoon.

quiz.prompt: You cannot meet Saturday morning. Which response keeps the conversation moving?
options: Estoy ocupado por la mañana. ¿Por la tarde?
quiz.answer: Estoy ocupado por la mañana. ¿Por la tarde?
quiz.explanation: It explains the conflict and immediately suggests another part of the day.

quiz.prompt: Carlos writes: ¿A las diez de la mañana? Which detail should you add to your plan?
options: Meet at ten in the morning
quiz.answer: Meet at ten in the morning
quiz.explanation: A las diez de la mañana gives the exact agreed meeting time.

quiz.prompt: Complete the plan from invitation to goodbye.
options: Sí, estoy libre. El sábado por la mañana. A las diez. Perfecto. Nos vemos.
Hola. Me llamo Tom. Soy de Canadá. Mucho gusto.
Esta es mi madre. Él es mi hermano. Tengo una hermana.
quiz.answer: Sí, estoy libre. El sábado por la mañana. A las diez. Perfecto. Nos vemos.
quiz.explanation: This response accepts, confirms the day and time, and closes the plan naturally.

flashcards.selectionRule: Only Tier 1 planning language and mission-critical chunks appear in the core deck.

speakingChallenge.prompt: Ask Carlos whether he is free this Saturday.
Say that you are free in the morning.
Confirm ten o’clock and close naturally.
speakingChallenge.exampleAnswer: A las diez. Perfecto. Nos vemos entonces.

speakingChallenge.prompt: Give a true day and part of the day when you are normally free.
speakingChallenge.exampleAnswer: Estoy libre el domingo por la tarde.

realLifeMission.title: Carlos Challenge: Weekend Plans
realLifeMission.mission: Arrange the market visit entirely in Spanish. Agree on a day, a part of the day, and a meeting time, then close the conversation naturally.
successCriteria: Respond to ¿Estás libre? with estoy libre or estoy ocupado/ocupada.
Choose por la mañana or por la tarde.
Confirm a las diez or respond to another simple time Carlos offers.
Close with nos vemos or nos vemos entonces.
realLifeMission.completionResponse: ¡Plan perfecto! You arranged a real outing in Spanish—from availability to goodbye.
realLifeMission.correctionRule: Celebrate the completed plan first. Correct only one day/time phrase that affects when the meeting happens.
realLifeMission.handoff: ¡Perfecto! Nos vemos el sábado a las diez. Antes de vernos, necesito comprar algo especial en el mercado... Mañana te cuento.

passportStamp.title: Weekend Plans
passportStamp.description: You read Carlos’s message and arranged a Saturday market visit in Spanish.
passportStamp.unlockCondition: Complete the Carlos Challenge for Lesson 4.

achievement.title: Plan Maker
achievement.description: Arrange your first day and meeting time entirely in Spanish.
